280	- **Helper accumulation**: <description, e.g. "13 helpers added across last 4 iters; 1 sorry closed">
281	- **Prover dispatch pattern**: <e.g. "1 of 3 ready files dispatched for 3 consecutive iters">
282	- **Recurring blockers**: <list or "none">
283	- **Avoidance patterns**: <list or "none" — name each: off-critical-path reclassification, consecutive plan-only iters, persistent deferral language, possible rotation churn>
284	- **Prover status pattern**: <e.g. "PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL">
285	- **Throughput**: ON_SCHEDULE | SLIPPING | OVER_BUDGET | ESTIMATE_FREE — <estimated K iters, elapsed K'>
286	- **Verdict**: CONVERGING | CHURNING | STUCK | UNCLEAR
287	- **Primary corrective** (if CHURNING/STUCK): <one of the actions above, with one paragraph of why>
288	- **Secondary correctives** (if applicable): <list>
289	
290	## PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity
291	
292	Independent of any route verdict.
293	
294	- **File count**: <N> (cap: <C>)
295	- **Ready but not dispatched**: <list of files with complete chapters and open sorries that are NOT in the proposal, or "none identified">
296	- **Over the cap**: yes | no
297	- **Under-dispatch finding**: yes | no — <if yes: gap size, how many consecutive iters>
298	- **Iter-over-iter trend**: <e.g. "1 → 1 → 1; consistently under-dispatching 3 ready files">
299	- **Verdict**: OK | OVER_CAP | UNDER_DISPATCH | BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS
300	  - OK: dispatch list is within cap, not under-dispatching ready files, and not growing while routes churn.
301	  - OVER_CAP: more files than the cap allows.
302	  - UNDER_DISPATCH: ≥3 ready files absent from the proposal, or consistently fewer than ready across ≥2 iters. Land in must-fix-this-iter.
303	  - BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS: file count growing iter over iter while route signals say CHURNING/STUCK.
304	
305	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if no CHURNING/STUCK verdicts AND no OVER_BUDGET/OVER_CAP/UNDER_DISPATCH/BLOAT findings -->
306	
307	Every CHURNING and every STUCK verdict lands here automatically, every OVER_BUDGET throughput finding (with `Iters left > 0`), and every OVER_CAP / UNDER_DISPATCH / BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS dispatch verdict. Do not under-classify.

closed-monoidal apparatus). The two new decls `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`
(L417) and `monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW` (L446) are permanent axiom-clean

- **(b) SMALLER**: supply `whiskerLeft` directly by a flatness-free sectionwise

# Mathlib Analogist Directive

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
ts-design206

## Design question
To equip the relative Picard functor with its abelian-group structure
(`AddCommGroup` on `Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`, i.e. the group law
"tensor product of line bundles"), the project is building the FULL
symmetric monoidal category structure on `Scheme.Modules X` (all
quasi-coherent modules), realised as `sheafification ∘
PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`. Closing that monoidal-category
instance has bottomed out in a verified-absent Mathlib instance
`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` (the relative module tensor over a
*varying* base ring), needed only to prove that sheafification inverts
`F ◁ g` (the `whiskerLeft` stability of the localizing morphism property).

**Is building the full `MonoidalCategory (Scheme.Modules X)` the right
Mathlib-aligned shape for obtaining only the group law on LINE BUNDLES
(invertible/locally-free-rank-1 sheaves), or does Mathlib have a lighter
idiom — a Picard group of invertible sheaves, the group of invertible
objects (`≅`-units) of a monoidal category, invertible-module units, etc.
— that yields the `AddCommGroup` (= `CommGroup` written additively) on
line-bundle iso-classes WITHOUT requiring the full monoidal (let alone
monoidal-closed) structure on all modules?**

## Project artifact(s) under question
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:113–150 —
  `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` (= `sheafification ∘ presheaf tensorObj`) and
  the `monoidalCategory` instance (L150, `:= sorry`).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:277–342 —
  `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`, `exists_tensorObj_inverse`,
  `addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` (L339) — the group-law cone whose terminal
  target is the `AddCommGroup`.
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:411–453 —
  `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`, `monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW` (the
  localization-transport infra that reduces the cone to `whiskerLeft`).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`:235 — the `addCommGroup`
  sorry this cone is meant to discharge (the relative Picard quotient group).

## Why now
Three consecutive net-0-sorry TS iters (−2, 0, 0); the lane has reached a
multi-file Mathlib infrastructure boundary (`MonoidalClosed
(PresheafOfModules R₀)`, verified absent) identical in class to the
blocker that paused the COE lane. Before committing a multi-file
Mathlib-PR-scale sub-lane to build that closed structure, I need to know
whether the project is over-building: the group law it actually needs is
just "tensor of two line bundles is a line bundle, with inverses" — a
`CommGroup` on Pic — which classically needs far less than a full
monoidal-closed category on all quasi-coherent modules.

## Hints (optional)
- Mathlib namespaces likely relevant: `CategoryTheory.Monoidal` (units /
  invertible objects — is there a `Monoidal....Grp` of `≅`-invertible
  objects?), `AlgebraicGeometry` Picard-group material if any,
  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.{Sheaf,Presheaf}`, invertible-module
  / `Module.Picard` if it exists, `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal`,
  `CategoryTheory.Sites.Monoidal` (`J.W.IsMonoidal` — ambient `⊗_ℤ`, NOT
  the relative tensor, already a dead-end).
- The line-bundle tensor preserves locally-trivial-rank-1 LOCALLY (where
  both are trivial), which `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial` already proves
  modulo `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. The question is whether the
  group-of-invertibles can be assembled from that local fact directly,
  bypassing the global `MonoidalCategory` instance.
- Already-exhausted dead-ends (do NOT recommend): `Sites/Monoidal.lean`'s
  `J.W.IsMonoidal` (wrong tensor); sectionwise presheaf-pullback
  strong-monoidal (absent); elementwise exactness (needs flatness).

## Severity expectation
high-stakes

# Strategy Auditor Directive

## Slug
rrfree206

## Iter
206

## The strategic question to audit

The project's stated goal is the existence of a Jacobian/Albanese object
for a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C/k`, routed through
`J := Pic⁰_{C/k}` (Kleiman §4–§5 + Nitsure §5 + Milne III §6). The project
currently treats **Riemann–Roch (Route C)** as a dependency of
`Pic_{C/k}` representability (the node A.2.c), and Route C is PAUSED by a
USER standing directive — which makes the unconditional goal unreachable
in the genus ≥ 1 case until the pause lifts.

**Audit this claim**: is `Pic_{C/k}` (or specifically `Pic⁰_{C/k}`)
representability genuinely dependent on Riemann–Roch, or is it RR-free?
Concretely, validate or refute each of the following against the actual
source PDFs/TeX:

1. **(RR-free bare representability)** Does the FGA/Grothendieck–Nitsure
   Quot-scheme construction of `Pic_{X/S}` representability invoke
   Riemann–Roch anywhere, or is bare representability of the Picard
   functor classically RR-free (RR entering only later, e.g. to compute
   dimensions or identify components by degree)? Read **Nitsure,
   "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes"** (the Quot/representability
   engine) and **Kleiman, "The Picard scheme" §4** (existence).

2. **(degree-0 component without RR)** The project defines
   `Pic⁰_{C/k} := PicScheme.degComp C 0` (the degree-0 component, Kleiman
   §6, Hilbert-polynomial open-closed decomposition). Identifying a
   component *by its degree* uses RR (the degree↔Hilbert-poly link, RR.1).
   Would defining `Pic⁰` instead as **the connected component through the
   trivial class `[O_C]`** (Kleiman §6 `ex:curves` / Milne III §1, the
   identity component) avoid the RR-dependent degree identification while
   yielding the SAME object for a smooth proper geom-integral curve?
   Read **Kleiman §6** (`ex:curves`, `rmk:curves`) and **Milne III §1
   (Thm 1.6)**.

3. **(downstream RR dependence)** Even if representability is RR-free, do
   the *downstream* nodes the project needs — the Albanese universal
   property (Milne III §6, Prop 6.1/6.4) and the Pic^d / divisor-map
   construction (Kleiman §5 + Milne III §4, the `Sym^d`/Abel map route) —
   independently require Riemann–Roch (e.g. via the genus formula, or
   `Sym^d C → Pic^d` surjectivity for `d ≥ 2g-1`)? Read **Milne III §4
   and §6** and **Kleiman §5**.

The decision at stake: if (1) holds and (2) yields an RR-free `Pic⁰`, and
(3) shows the Albanese UP can be reached RR-free (or only the genus
formula needs RR), then the project may be able to **cut Riemann–Roch off
the genus ≥ 1 critical path**, reaching the unconditional goal without
lifting the Route C pause. That is a major re-route — I must not commit it
on my own recollection; your audit against the actual sources is the gate.

## What to read (sources in references/)
- `references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.pdf` (and `-src/*.tex`) — Quot/Hilbert
  construction; representability engine.
- `references/kleiman-picard.pdf` (and `-src/*.tex`) — §4 existence, §5
  Pic⁰/Jacobian (pp.36–51), §6 ex:curves/rmk:curves.
- `references/abelian-varieties.pdf` (Milne) — III §1 (Thm 1.6 identity
  component), III §4 (Sym^d/Abel), III §6 (Prop 6.1/6.4 Albanese UP).

Use the `.md` pointer cards only to locate sections; base every finding on
the raw PDF/TeX text and quote the operative sentences.

## What to report
For each of the three sub-questions: VALIDATED / REFUTED / PARTIAL, with
the verbatim source sentence(s) that decide it and the section/page. Then
a bottom-line: can RR be cut from the genus ≥ 1 critical path for the
*representability + Pic⁰-identification* layer (yes/no/partial), and which
downstream nodes (if any) still force RR. Flag any silent assumption the
sources make that the project's RR-free re-route would violate (e.g. a
properness/projectivity hypothesis, a `Sym^d` surjectivity that needs
`d ≥ 2g-1`, a base-point/section requirement).

1	# Mathlib Analogist Report
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	ts-design206
8	
9	## Iteration
10	206
11	
12	## Question
13	Is building the full `MonoidalCategory (Scheme.Modules X)` (whose only remaining gap is the
14	verified-absent `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)`, needed for `whiskerLeft` stability of the
15	sheafification-localizing morphism property) the right Mathlib-aligned shape for obtaining only the
16	group law on LINE BUNDLES, or does Mathlib have a lighter idiom that yields the `AddCommGroup` on
17	line-bundle iso-classes WITHOUT the full monoidal (let alone monoidal-closed) structure on all modules?
18	
19	## Verdicts (summary)
20	
21	| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
22	|---|---|---|
23	| A. Full `MonoidalCategory`/`MonoidalClosed` on **all** modules as the group-law substrate | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB (restrict to flat/line-bundle scope) | critical |
24	| B. Group-law shape: hand-rolled quotient vs `Units (Skeleton C)` + invertibility predicate | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB / NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL | major |
25	
26	## Headline
27	
28	**The project is over-building.** Mathlib's Picard group `CommRing.Pic R`
29	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`) is `Units (Skeleton (ModuleCat R))` over a **fixed** ring — it
30	uses `ModuleCat.monoidalCategory`/`SymmetricCategory` (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.*`)
31	and needs **no sheafification, no localization, and no `MonoidalClosed`**. The invertibility carrier is a
32	Prop predicate `Module.Invertible R M` (PicardGroup.lean:78) — exactly what the project's
33	`IsLocallyTrivial` is described as ("the project-side stand-in for the missing Mathlib `IsInvertible`
34	predicate", LineBundlePullback.lean:106). The expensive `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` is only
35	forced because the project tries to prove `whiskerLeft : W g → W (F ◁ g)` for an **arbitrary** `F`. The
36	consumer only tensors **line bundles**, which are **flat** (in Mathlib `Module.Invertible ⇒ Projective`,
37	PicardGroup.lean:195–196), and for flat `F` the `whiskerLeft` fact is ordinary exactness —
38	`Module.Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap` (used inside PicardGroup.lean:205–207; packaged as
39	`Module.Invertible.lTensor_bijective_iff`, PicardGroup.lean:223–229) plus right-exactness — **not**
40	internal-hom machinery. The directive's "elementwise exactness (needs flatness)" dead-end was a dead-end
41	only because the target was all modules; restricting scope to line bundles makes that flatness automatic.
42	
43	## Must-fix-this-iter
44	
45	- **Decision A — abandon the all-modules monoidal-closed route.** `TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:150
46	  (`monoidalCategory := sorry`) and the `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`/`monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW`
47	  transport (L399–454) that reduces the substrate to `whiskerLeft` for ARBITRARY `F` are aimed at the
48	  wrong target. The group law never consumes coherence (pentagon/triangle/hexagon) or closedness — the
49	  group axioms on iso-classes are *propositions* `Nonempty (… ≅ …)`. Do **not** commit a multi-file
50	  `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` sub-lane. Re-scope `whiskerLeft`/`tensorObj_restrict_iso`
51	  (L249) to FLAT `F` (line bundles), where it is elementary flat-exactness already in Mathlib.
52	  Cost of not doing so: a verified-absent, Mathlib-PR-scale internal-hom-over-varying-ring build, for
53	  structure the consumer discards.
54	
55	## Major
56	
57	- **Decision B — mirror `Units (Skeleton …)`, do not hand-roll the quotient group from scratch.**
58	  `addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` (`TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:339) and the RelPicFunctor L245–264 plan rebuild
59	  the quotient `AddCommGroup` via a global monoidal category + dual + `QuotientAddGroup` transport — a
60	  parallel API to Mathlib's `instCommGroupPic` = `Units` of the skeleton `CommMonoid`
61	  (`CategoryTheory.Skeleton.instCommMonoid`). Scheme-level Pic is genuinely absent from Mathlib
62	  (PicardGroup.lean TODO:59 lists "connect to invertible sheaves" as unbuilt), so a project construction
63	  is unavoidable — but it should mirror the *shape*: keep `IsLocallyTrivial` as the
64	  `Module.Invertible`-style Prop, and obtain the group as iso-classes/units of the **line-bundle
65	  subcategory**, assembled from existence-of-iso lemmas. Two concrete realizations, both bypassing the
66	  blocker:
67	    1. **Minimal (recommended first):** prove four existence-of-iso lemmas on `OnProduct` — assoc
68	       `(L⊗M)⊗N ≅ L⊗(M⊗N)`, unit `O⊗L ≅ L`, comm `L⊗M ≅ M⊗L`, inverse `exists_tensorObj_inverse`
69	       (L300, have it as scaffold) — each via the flat-restricted `tensorObj_restrict_iso`, then build
70	       `AddCommGroup` on the quotient directly. No `MonoidalCategory` instance, no coherence, no closed
71	       structure.
72	    2. **Packaged (cleaner long-term):** give the full subcategory of line bundles a symmetric monoidal
73	       structure (flat ⇒ `whiskerLeft` holds elementwise) and set `Pic := Units (Skeleton (LineBundles))`
74	       — Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic` idiom transported to schemes.
75	
76	## Informational
77	
78	- The one ingredient that is genuinely required even for line bundles is a **global** associator/unit
79	  iso (`tensorObj_restrict_iso`): local triviality alone does NOT give it — two sheaves both locally `≅ O`
80	  need not be globally iso (e.g. `O(1)` vs `O`). But for line bundles that global iso comes from the flat
81	  `whiskerLeft` fact, which is in Mathlib; it does not come from `MonoidalClosed`. So the substrate does
82	  not collapse to nothing — it collapses to flat-exactness.
83	- `Module.Invertible` instances confirm the rigidity that makes this work: invertible/line-bundle objects
84	  are dualizable (the dual `Mᵛ` is the inverse, `Pic.mk_dual`), so the relevant internal hom *of the
85	  object* exists even though the ambient category is not monoidal-closed — another way to see the
86	  closed-structure requirement is spurious for this lane.
87	
88	## Persistent file
89	- `analogies/ts-design206.md` — design rationale (two decisions, citations, the flat-scope pivot) for future iters.
90	
91	Overall verdict: the full `MonoidalCategory`/`MonoidalClosed`-on-all-modules path is over-building — the
92	line-bundle group law is Mathlib's `Units (Skeleton …)`/`Module.Invertible` idiom, and its one hard
93	ingredient reduces to elementary flat-exactness once scoped to line bundles, so the verified-absent
94	`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` sub-lane should not be opened.
95

1	# Strategy Audit Report: RR-Free Representability of Pic⁰ (Iter 206)
2	
3	**Slug:** rrfree206  
4	**Date:** 2026-05-29  
5	**Sources read:** Kleiman `kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex` (full §4, §5, §6); Nitsure `nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex` (§5 in full); Milne `abelian-varieties.pdf` (Chapter III §1–§6, pp. 85–110).
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Sub-question 1: RR-free bare representability
10	
11	**Verdict: VALIDATED**
12	
13	### Finding
14	
15	The FGA/Grothendieck–Nitsure construction of `Pic_{X/S}` representability invokes **zero** Riemann–Roch at any point. This is confirmed in both sources:
16	
17	**Nitsure (§5, Construction of Quot Schemes, pp. 23–28):** A full-text grep for "Riemann" in `nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex` returns **no matches**. The construction uses: Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity (§2), base-change for flat sheaves (§3), flattening stratification (§4), embedding of Quot into a Grassmannian via `m`-regular global sections, and the valuative criterion for properness. Nothing close to RR appears.
18	
19	**Kleiman §4 (`th:main`, L2155–2417):** RR appears in Kleiman's paper only in (a) the historical introduction (equation `\ref{equation:RR}`, L517, the classical Abel–Roch formula) and (b) Appendix B (`prp:RR`, L6134, "Riemann–Roch for surfaces" in the intersection-theory appendix). Neither appears in §4. The proof of `th:main` decomposes `P = Pic_{(X/S)_et}` into open–closed pieces by Hilbert polynomial:
20	
21	> *"Given a polynomial φ ∈ ℚ[n], let P^φ ⊂ P be the étale subsheaf associated to the presheaf whose T-points are represented by the invertible sheaves ℒ on X_T such that* χ(X_t, ℒ^{-1}_t(n)) = φ(n) *for all t ∈ T."* (L2188–2192)
22	
23	This is χ-based, not degree-based; no Hilbert–polynomial = degree-formula is invoked. The proof then uses vanishing-of-higher-direct-images conditions (`eq:4b`/`eq:4c`), the Hilbert scheme representability of `Div_{X/S}`, the linear series `IP(Q)`, the surjectivity of `Z → P₀^{φ₀}` via étale sections (using EGAIV4 17.16.3), and the patching lemma `lm:qt`. No step invokes RR.
24	
25	**Conclusion:** Bare representability of `Pic_{X/S}` is classically RR-free. RR enters the Picard-scheme story only later: to compute dimensions (`thm:tgtsp` gives `T_0 Pic = H^1(O_X)` — also RR-free), to identify components by degree, or to prove properties of the Sym^d–Abel route.
26	
27	---
28	
29	## Sub-question 2: Degree-0 component without RR
30	
31	**Verdict: VALIDATED (with one caveat about the equivalence proof)**
32	
33	### Finding A — defining `Pic⁰ := Pic^z` is RR-free
34	
35	Kleiman §5 ("The connected component of the identity," L2836–4041) defines `Pic^z_{X/S}` as the connected component of the identity element and proves:
36	
37	> *"It is remarkable how much we can prove about* `IPicz_{X/S}` *formally, or nearly so, from general principles. Notably, we can do without the finiteness theorems proved in the next section."* (L2843–2845)
38	
39	Key results, all RR-free:
40	- **`prp:pic0`** (L2921–2933): For `S = Spec k`, if `Pic_{X/k}` exists, then `Pic^z_{X/k}` is an open-and-closed group subscheme of finite type, geometrically irreducible, and stable under field extension. Proof cites only `lem:agps` (general group scheme theory).
41	- **`th:qpp&p`** (L2935–2984): If X/k is projective and geometrically integral, then `Pic^z_{X/k}` exists and is quasi-projective. If also geometrically normal, it is projective. Proof: quasi-projectivity from `ex:q-proj`; projectivity from the Chevalley–Rosenlicht structure theorem (no RR). For smooth C/k, geometrically normal follows from smoothness.
42	
43	Defining `Pic^0 := Pic^z_{X/k}` (the identity component) is therefore RR-free.
44	
45	### Finding B — the equivalence `Pic^z = Pic^0_{degree}` does require RR
46	
47	Kleiman **`ex:curves`** (L4661–4680) sets up `Pic^m_{X/S}` as the degree-m stratum and asks the reader to:
48	
49	> *"Show there is no abuse of notation: the fiber of* `IPicz_{X/S}` *over s ∈ S is the connected component of* 0 ∈ `IPic_{X_s/k_s}`.*"*
50	
51	This is a *proof obligation*, not a definition. Milne (p. 88) makes the required ingredient explicit:
52	
53	> *"and the Riemann-Roch theorem says that* χ(C, ℒⁿ) = n deg(ℒ) + 1 − g. *This gives a more canonical description of deg(ℒ): when* χ(C, ℒⁿ) *is written as a polynomial in n, deg(ℒ) is the leading coefficient."*
54	
55	This RR formula is what links the Hilbert-polynomial decomposition (which Kleiman §4 uses) to the degree-grading. Specifically:
56	- `Pic^z ⊆ Pic^0_{degree}` follows because `O_C` (degree 0) lies in the identity component, and the degree function is locally constant by this RR formula.
57	- `Pic^0_{degree}` is connected (= `Pic^z`) follows from the absence of torsion for integral curves (`ex:curves` also asks to show `Pic^z = Pic^τ`).
58	
59	**Conclusion for sub-question 2:** The project CAN define `Pic^0 := Pic^z` (identity component) — this is fully RR-free. Kleiman §5 constructs and characterises `Pic^z` without RR. The *proof* that this equals the degree-0 stratum requires RR (via Milne p. 88), but this proof is not needed to *have* and *use* `Pic^z` as the Jacobian. The project must **not** prove or use the statement "`PicScheme.degComp C 0 = Pic^z`" in the genus ≥ 1 arm if it wants to stay RR-free — instead it should directly define `Pic^0 := Pic^z` throughout.
60	
61	**Silent assumption flagged:** If the project's current Lean definition is `Pic⁰ := PicScheme.degComp C 0`, switching to `Pic^z` is a definitional change, not just a proof change. The degree-zero characterisation and the identity-component characterisation are provably equivalent (for smooth proper geom-integral curves), but that proof requires RR.
62	
63	---
64	
65	## Sub-question 3: Downstream RR dependence
66	
67	**Verdict: PARTIAL — Milne's Sym^d route forces RR; Kleiman's Albanese route avoids it with a caveat**
68	
69	### Finding A — Milne's Sym^d/Abel construction route forces RR at multiple places
70	
71	**Milne III §4, Proposition 4.2(b), p. 100** (constructing a section of `Div^r_C → P^r_C`):
72	
73	> *"Let ℒ be an invertible sheaf on C × T representing an element of P^γ(T). Then h⁰(ℒ_t ⊗ ℒ_γ⁻¹) = 1 for all t, **and the Riemann-Roch theorem shows that h¹(D_t − D_γ) = 0 for all t**."*
74	
75	This is the key step that produces a section, making `P^r_C` representable. **Explicit RR.**
76	
77	**Milne III §5, Lemma 5.2(b), p. 102** (open cover of C^{(r)} needed for Theorem 5.1):
78	
79	> *"The Riemann-Roch theorem now shows that h⁰(Σ Pᵢ) = r + (1 − g) + (g − r) = 1 for all (P₁,...,P_r) in U."*
80	
81	**Explicit RR.** This is used to prove the Abel map f^{(r)}: C^{(r)} → J is birational.
82	
83	**Milne III §5, Theorem 5.1 proof, p. 103** (birationality of C^{(g)} → J):
84	
85	> *"The Riemann-Roch theorem says precisely that these two numbers are equal, and so completes the proof."*
86	
87	**Explicit RR.** The two numbers are dim Im(di)_D = |D| and dim Ker(df^{(r)})_D = g − h⁰(Ω(−D)).
88	
89	**Milne III §6, Proposition 6.1, p. 104** (Albanese UP): The proof uses the rational map ψ̂: J → A obtained by factoring (P₁,...,P_g) ↦ Σ ψ(Pᵢ) through C^{(g)}, then invoking (I 3.2) to extend. The factoring through J uses the birational map f^{(g)}: C^{(g)} → J (Theorem 5.1), which relies on RR as above. **Indirect RR.**
90	
91	**Milne III §1, p. 87** (surjectivity of Abel map, used as prerequisite):
92	
93	> *"For a divisor of degree r, the Riemann-Roch theorem says that ℓ(D) = r + 1 − g + ℓ(K − D) ... every divisor class of degree r contains an effective divisor, and so the map φ: {effective divisors of degree r} → Pic^r(C), D ↦ [D] is surjective when r > 2g − 2."*
94	
95	**Explicit RR.** This surjectivity (for d ≥ 2g−1) is what makes the Sym^d/Abel route work. The threshold d ≥ 2g−1 is precisely RR-controlled.
96	
97	### Finding B — Kleiman's Albanese route (`rmk:Alb`) is RR-free for the universal property itself
98	
99	Kleiman `rmk:Alb` (L3960–3988) gives a self-contained Albanese universal property for `Pic^z(X)` using only the Comparison Theorem and the group-scheme structure of `Pic^z`:
100	
101	> *"Consider a map a: B^* → P such that a(0) = 0. By the Comparison Theorem, Theorem th:cmp, a corresponds to an invertible sheaf ℒ on X × B^* such that ξ^*ℒ ≅ O_X. ... Then ℒ defines a map b: X → B such that b(x) = 0. Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every such b arises from a unique map a: B^* → Pic_{X/k} such that a(0) = 0. ... In particular, 1_P corresponds to a natural map u: X → A such that u(x) = 0, and every map b: X → B factors uniquely through u."*
102	
103	This proof uses:
104	- `th:cmp` (Comparison Theorem, L1384–1399): RR-free; it equates the four Picard functors under the hypothesis O_S ≅ f_*O_X.
105	- `th:qpp&p` (projectivity of `Pic^z`, RR-free).
106	- The duality between `B` and `B^* = Pic^z(B)` for an abelian variety B (from `rmk:Ablsch`).
107	
108	The Albanese variety in Kleiman's route is A = `Pic^z(Pic^z(C)) = J^∨` (the dual Jacobian), and the universal Albanese map is u: C → J^∨. **This step is RR-free.**
109	
110	### Caveat: autoduality J ≅ J^∨ may force RR
111	
112	To identify `J^∨` (Kleiman's Albanese output) with `J = Pic^z(C)` (the Jacobian), one needs autoduality. Kleiman `rmk:Jac` (L3990–4016) cites [EGK, Thm 2.1] for the autoduality isomorphism A_L^*: Ĵ ≅ J. The degree-1 invertible sheaf ℒ required for the Abel map A_L requires either C(k) ≠ ∅ or an fppf cover. The [EGK] autoduality proof (Esteves–Gagné–Kleiman) is for a compactified Jacobian and is independent of RR at the level of the abstract statement, though the classical proof (via degree of the polarization = 1 using θ-divisor) typically invokes RR for abelian varieties.
113	
114	**If the project's goal is stated as "J^∨ is the Albanese variety of C" (rather than "J = Albanese(C)"), the Kleiman `rmk:Alb` route is fully RR-free.** If full self-duality J ≅ J^∨ is needed, this is a separate task that may require additional machinery beyond Route C.
115	
116	---
117	
118	## Bottom Line
119	
120	### Can RR be cut from the genus ≥ 1 critical path for representability + Pic⁰-identification?
121	
122	**YES, for the representability and Pic⁰-definition layer:**
123	- `Pic_{X/S}` representability (Kleiman §4 / Nitsure §5): **fully RR-free.**
124	- `Pic^z_{X/S}` = identity component: **fully RR-free** (Kleiman §5).
125	- Both `Pic_{X/S}` and `Pic^z_{X/S}` are projective abelian schemes for smooth proper geom-integral C/k: **fully RR-free.**
126	- The Abel map f^P: C → J (defined via divisorial correspondences, Kleiman `rmk:Jac`): **RR-free** given a degree-1 invertible sheaf (equivalently, a rational point P ∈ C(k)).
127	
128	**PARTIAL, for the downstream Albanese UP:**
129	- **Milne's Sym^d route** (currently in the project via Route C) forces RR at Prop 4.2(b), Lemma 5.2(b), Theorem 5.1, and indirectly at Prop 6.1.
130	- **Kleiman's `rmk:Alb` route** gives an RR-free Albanese universal property with output A = J^∨. Identifying J ≅ J^∨ (autoduality) is a further step that may or may not need RR depending on the proof path.
131	
132	### Which downstream nodes still force RR
133	
134	| Node | RR forced? | Source |
135	|------|-----------|--------|
136	| Bare representability of `Pic_{X/S}` | **No** | Kleiman §4, Nitsure §5 |
137	| `Pic^z` = connected component of identity | **No** | Kleiman §5 |
138	| Projectivity of `Pic^z` (genus g curve → abelian variety) | **No** | Kleiman `th:qpp&p` |
139	| Abel map f^P: C → J | **No** (needs rational point) | Kleiman `rmk:Jac` |
140	| Sym^d surjectivity (`C^{(r)} → Pic^r` for r > 2g−2) | **Yes** | Milne III §1 p.87 |
141	| Section construction for `Div^r_C → P^r_C` | **Yes** | Milne III §4 Prop 4.2(b) |
142	| Birationality of C^{(g)} → J | **Yes** | Milne III §5 Thm 5.1 |
143	| Albanese UP via Sym^d (Milne Prop 6.1) | **Yes (indirect)** | Milne III §6 Prop 6.1 |
144	| Albanese UP via Comparison Thm (Kleiman `rmk:Alb`) | **No** (output = J^∨) | Kleiman §5 `rmk:Alb` |
145	| Autoduality J ≅ J^∨ (to recover J as Albanese) | **Likely yes** | Kleiman `rmk:Jac` / [EGK] |
146	| Genus formula dim J = g | **Yes** | Milne III Prop 2.1; uses RR (H^1(O_C)≅T_0J) |
147	
148	### Silent assumptions the re-route must not violate
149	
150	1. **Projectivity, not merely properness.** Kleiman `th:main` and `th:qpp&p` require X/S *projective* (Zariski locally). A smooth proper curve over a field is projective, so this is satisfied, but must be stated.
151	
152	2. **Geometric normality for projectivity of `Pic^z`.** Kleiman `th:qpp&p` requires X/k *geometrically normal* for `Pic^z` to be projective (not just quasi-projective). Smoothness implies normality, but this hypothesis must be tracked.
153	
154	3. **k-rational point requirement.** Kleiman's `rmk:Alb` requires a base point x ∈ X(k). Milne Prop 6.1 requires P ∈ C(k). Milne's Proposition 1.14 handles the case C(k) = ∅ by a Galois descent argument (no RR involved). The project must flag whether C is assumed to have a rational point.
155	
156	4. **Sym^d surjectivity threshold.** If the project uses any Sym^d argument at degree d ≥ 2g−1 to prove surjectivity, it is invoking RR (Milne III §1 p.87). This threshold is hard-wired into Milne's construction. The Kleiman `rmk:Alb` alternative avoids it entirely.
157	
158	5. **Definitional gap.** The project currently uses `PicScheme.degComp C 0` as Pic⁰. Switching to `Pic^z` (identity component) is a **definitional change** in Lean. It will require re-routing the definition through `lem:agps`/`prp:pic0` (identity-component construction of group schemes), not through the Hilbert-polynomial stratum. The equivalence `Pic^z = PicScheme.degComp C 0` is a theorem (requiring RR) that should be deferred, not a tautology.
159	
160	---
161	
162	## Recommendation
163	
164	The re-route is mathematically sound at the representability + Pic⁰-definition layer:
165	
166	> **Cut Route C from the critical path for `Pic_{X/S}` representability and `Pic^z` construction.** Both are RR-free per Kleiman §4–§5 and Nitsure §5.
167	
168	> **Switch the Lean definition of `Pic⁰` from `PicScheme.degComp C 0` to `IPic^z_{C/k}` (identity component).** This removes the RR-dependent identification of Hilbert-polynomial strata with degree-zero bundles.
169	
170	> **For the Albanese UP:** Replace Milne's Sym^d route (Prop 6.1 via Thm 5.1) with Kleiman's `rmk:Alb` route, which gives A = J^∨ as the Albanese object. The claim that J = J^∨ (self-duality) is the one remaining node that likely requires either RR or a separate investment in the autoduality theory (cite [EGK] / Mumford §20, or defer).
171	
172	> **For the genus formula** dim J = g: this requires RR (or Milne Prop 2.1 which uses the identification T_0(J) = H^1(O_C)); defer to Route C or accept as a sorried lemma until RR is available.
173

1	# Analogy: lighter idiom for the line-bundle group law (vs full MonoidalCategory on all modules)
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	ts-design206
8	
9	## Iteration
10	206
11	
12	## Question
13	To equip the relative Picard functor with its `AddCommGroup` (the group law
14	"tensor of line bundles") the project is building the FULL symmetric monoidal
15	category `Scheme.Modules X` (= `sheafification ∘ PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`),
16	which has bottomed out in the verified-absent `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)`
17	(needed for the `whiskerLeft` stability of the localizing morphism property).
18	Is the full `MonoidalCategory (Scheme.Modules X)` the right Mathlib-aligned shape
19	for obtaining only the group law on LINE BUNDLES, or does Mathlib have a lighter
20	idiom that yields the `AddCommGroup` on line-bundle iso-classes WITHOUT the full
21	monoidal (let alone monoidal-closed) structure on all modules?
22	
23	## Project artifact(s)
24	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:113–152 — `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj`
25	  and the `monoidalCategory : MonoidalCategory X.Modules` instance (`:= sorry`).
26	- `TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:249–304 — `tensorObj_restrict_iso`, `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`,
27	  `exists_tensorObj_inverse` (the line-bundle cone).
28	- `TensorObjSubstrate.lean`:399–454 — `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft` /
29	  `monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW`: the transport reducing the whole substrate to the
30	  lone `whiskerLeft` fact `W g → W (F ◁ g)` for ALL `F`.
31	- `RelPicFunctor.lean`:235–269 — the `addCommGroup` sorry the cone discharges.
32	- `LineBundlePullback.lean`:115–131 — `IsLocallyTrivial` (the project's self-described
33	  "stand-in for the missing Mathlib `IsInvertible` predicate") and `OnProduct` carrier.
34	
35	## Decisions identified
36	
37	### Decision A: full `MonoidalCategory (Scheme.Modules X)` (→ `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)`) as the substrate for the group law
38	
39	- **Mathlib idiom**. Mathlib's Picard group is `CommRing.Pic R := Shrink (Skeleton (SemimoduleCat R))ˣ`
40	  with the `CommGroup` transported from `Units` of the skeleton `CommMonoid`
41	  (`Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`, `CommRing.Pic` def:407, `instCommGroupPic`:412;
42	  `CategoryTheory.Skeleton.instCommMonoid` from `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Skeleton`).
43	  The invertibility carrier is a **Prop predicate** `Module.Invertible R M`
44	  (PicardGroup.lean:78), NOT a bundled monoidal object. Crucially the monoidal
45	  category underlying the skeleton there is `ModuleCat R` over a **fixed** ring
46	  (`ModuleCat.monoidalCategory`, `ModuleCat.MonoidalCategory.symmetricCategory`,
47	  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.{Basic,Symmetric}`) — which needs **no**
48	  sheafification, localization, or `MonoidalClosed`.
49	- **Project's path**. Build `MonoidalCategory` on ALL quasi-coherent modules
50	  `Scheme.Modules X`, whose only remaining gap is `whiskerLeft : W g → W (F ◁ g)`
51	  for an ARBITRARY presheaf-of-modules `F`, provable (à la Mathlib's
52	  `GrothendieckTopology.W.whiskerLeft`) only through `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)`
53	  — verified absent and a multi-file Mathlib-PR-scale build.
54	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost (over-building). The consumer (`RelPicFunctor`
55	  `addCommGroup`) only ever tensors **line bundles** (`IsLocallyTrivial`, locally free
56	  rank 1). Line bundles are **flat** — in Mathlib, `Module.Invertible R M` already
57	  yields `instance : Module.Finite`, `instance : Projective` (PicardGroup.lean:195–196),
58	  hence flat. For a FLAT `F`, `F ◁ g` is locally bijective whenever `g` is, by ordinary
59	  exactness: flat ⇒ `lTensor` preserves injectivity
60	  (`Module.Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap`, used in PicardGroup.lean:205–207;
61	  packaged as `Module.Invertible.lTensor_bijective_iff`, PicardGroup.lean:223–229), and
62	  `⊗` is always right-exact so it preserves surjectivity. So the ENTIRE reason the project
63	  needs `MonoidalClosed` — that `whiskerLeft` is false elementwise for non-flat `F` — evaporates
64	  once the scope is the flat/line-bundle subcategory. The directive's "elementwise exactness
65	  (needs flatness)" dead-end was a dead-end ONLY because the target was all modules; the
66	  flatness it "needs" is automatic for line bundles.
67	- **Cost of divergence**: a verified-absent multi-file `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)`
68	  build (the relative internal hom over a varying base ring; the module analogue of
69	  `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Monoidal`), to obtain coherence (pentagon/triangle/hexagon
70	  + monoidal-closedness) that the group law never consumes — the group axioms on iso-classes
71	  are *propositions* (`Nonempty (… ≅ …)`), insensitive to coherence.
72	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB — abandon the all-modules monoidal-closed route; build the
73	  group law on the line-bundle (flat) subcategory, where `whiskerLeft`/`tensorObj_restrict_iso`
74	  reduce to elementary flat-exactness already in Mathlib.
75	
76	### Decision B: shape of the group-law assembly (`addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` hand-roll vs `Units ∘ Skeleton` + invertibility predicate)
77	
78	- **Mathlib idiom**. `Pic = Units (Skeleton C)` for a braided monoidal `C`
79	  (`CommRing.Pic`, `instCommGroupPic`); the iso-class arithmetic is the family
80	  `Pic.mk_tensor` ([M⊗N]=[M]·[N]), `Pic.mk_dual` ([Mᵛ]=[M]⁻¹), `mul_eq_tensor`,
81	  `mk_eq_one_iff_free` (PicardGroup.lean:480–496) — i.e. pure *existence-of-iso* facts.
82	  The invertibility carrier is the Prop `Module.Invertible`, exactly mirrored by the
83	  project's `IsLocallyTrivial` (LineBundlePullback.lean:106 says so verbatim).
84	- **Project's path**. `addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` (TensorObjSubstrate.lean:339) and the
85	  RelPicFunctor L245–264 plan hand-build the quotient `AddCommGroup` from a global monoidal
86	  category + dual + `QuotientAddGroup` transport — a parallel API to `Units (Skeleton …)`.
87	- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent, but only because scheme-level Pic is genuinely not in Mathlib
88	  (PicardGroup.lean TODO:59 explicitly lists "connect to invertible sheaves" as unbuilt). Some
89	  project construction is unavoidable; the alignment point is to mirror the *shape*: a Prop
90	  invertibility predicate (`IsLocallyTrivial`, have it) + the group as units/iso-classes of the
91	  **line-bundle** subcategory, assembled from the four existence-of-iso lemmas
92	  (assoc/unit/braiding/inverse) — NOT from a coherent monoidal category on all modules.
93	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB (shape), NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (scheme Pic itself is absent;
94	  the project supplies it, lightly).
95	
96	## Recommendation
97	
98	Stop building `MonoidalCategory (Scheme.Modules X)` and `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)`.
99	The relative Picard group law needs only the *group axioms on iso-classes of line bundles*, which
100	are propositions (`Nonempty (… ≅ …)`) and therefore require no coherence and no closed structure —
101	just four existence-of-iso lemmas (associativity, unit `O⊗L≅L`, commutativity, and inverse
102	`exists_tensorObj_inverse`) plus well-definedness (`tensorObjIsoOfIso`, already have). The single
103	hard ingredient `tensorObj_restrict_iso` / `whiskerLeft` is genuinely needed even for line bundles
104	(local triviality alone does NOT give a *global* associator), BUT only for FLAT `F` = line bundles,
105	where it is elementary exactness (`Module.Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap` +
106	right-exactness), not `MonoidalClosed`. Two realizations, both bypassing the blocker:
107	(1) **Minimal (recommended first):** prove the four iso lemmas on `OnProduct` directly via the
108	flat-restricted `whiskerLeft`, then assemble `AddCommGroup` on the quotient — no monoidal category
109	at all. (2) **Packaged (cleaner long-term):** equip the *full subcategory of line bundles* with a
110	(symmetric) monoidal structure (flat ⇒ `whiskerLeft` elementwise) and define
111	`Pic := Units (Skeleton (LineBundles X))`, exactly Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic` idiom transported to
112	schemes. Either way `IsLocallyTrivial` stays as the `Module.Invertible`-shaped predicate it already
113	mirrors. Do NOT reopen elementwise exactness for arbitrary `F` (the real dead-end); the move is to
114	change the *scope* to flat objects so the flatness hypothesis is free.
115

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-200). This chapter records the dedicated substrate
5	% on which the abelian-group instance of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
6	% (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) rests. The genuine residual
7	% sorry at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} L235 is
8	% the abelian-group law on the relative Picard quotient
9	% \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\); descending tensor product of
10	% line bundles to that quotient requires a tensor-product operation on
11	% the underlying carrier and an inverse for invertibles, neither of
12	% which is presently exposed at the \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level.
13	% Mathlib at the pinned commit ships
14	% \texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} (a tensor product at
15	% the level of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)), but does not lift this
16	% to a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\). The
17	% chapter describes the substrate
18	% \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\), records the lift
19	% through the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) carrier used by
20	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, and pins the consumer
21	% instance back into \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The chapter is
22	% project-side and self-contained: dropping a Mathlib upstream PR is
23	% acceptable but downstream work does NOT wait for the PR to land.
24	
25	% NOTE (planner directive iter-200). The chapter has three
26	% responsibilities. First, it records what the Mathlib API surface
27	% currently exposes at the relevant abstraction levels, what is
28	% missing, and at which abstraction the project-side construction must
29	% land. Second, it spells out the construction of
30	% \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) as a binary operation
31	% on the category \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), its functoriality, and
32	% the monoidal-category axioms it has to satisfy in order for the
33	% downstream Picard-quotient consumer to work. Third, it spells out
34	% the canonical lift through the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)
35	% carrier and pins the consumer abelian-group instance on the relative
36	% Picard quotient back into chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
37	
38	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
39	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
40	
41	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
42	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
43	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
44	\[
45	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
46	  \qquad
47	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
48	\]
49	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
50	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
51	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
52	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
53	
54	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
55	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
56	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
57	% groups)
58	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
59	% SOURCE QUOTE:
60	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
61	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
62	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
63	% abelian groups defined by the formula
64	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
65	% \end{dfn}
66	%  [\dots]
67	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
68	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
69	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
70	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
71	% topologies  by
72	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
73	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
74	%  \end{dfn}"
75	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
76	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
77	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
78	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
79	homomorphisms.}
80	
81	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
82	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
83	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
84	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
85	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
86	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
87	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
88	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
89	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
90	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
91	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
92	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
93	structure. This is the content of
94	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
95	
96	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
97	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
98	\[
99	  \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
100	  \;:=\; \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
101	\]
102	on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three ingredients on the
103	Lean carrier:
104	\begin{enumerate}
105	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
106	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
107	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\);
108	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
109	    for \(\otimes\);
110	  \item an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects,
111	    i.e.\ the dual \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L},
112	    \mathcal{O}_X)\) of an invertible sheaf.
113	\end{enumerate}
114	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
115	available; (2) and (3) are present as scheme-level objects, but the
116	binary operation in (1) that ties them together at the
117	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level is missing. This chapter records the
118	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) +
119	(3) into a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
120	
121	\section{Mathlib API survey}
122	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
123	
124	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
125	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
128	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
129	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
130	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
131	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
132	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
133	sectionwise tensor product
134	\[
135	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
136	\]
137	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
138	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
139	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
140	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
141	
142	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
144	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
145	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
146	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
147	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
148	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
149	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
150	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
151	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
152	tensor-product operation
153	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
154	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
155	
156	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
157	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
158	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
159	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
160	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
161	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) together with associator,
162	unitor, and braiding data. The project's policy on this gap is
163	\emph{project-side}: the substrate is built inside
164	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (a fresh file, sketched
165	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\), is the
166	natural home) and the consumer instance is closed against it. A
167	parallel Mathlib upstream PR with the same content is welcome but not
168	on the critical path for the project; the consumer is closed against
169	the project-side substrate independently of any upstream timing.
170	
171	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
172	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	
176	\leanok
177	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
178	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
180	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
181	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
182	  \[
183	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
184	  \]
185	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
186	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
187	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
188	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
189	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
190	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
191	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
192	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
193	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
194	  canonical compatibility
195	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
196	     \;\cong\;
197	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
198	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
199	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
200	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
201	
202	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
203	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
204	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
205	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
206	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
207	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
208	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
216	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
218	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
219	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
220	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
221	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
222	  \[
223	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
224	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
225	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
226	  \]
227	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
228	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
229	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
230	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
231	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
232	  isomorphisms
233	  \[
234	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
235	    \qquad
236	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	  \]
238	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
239	  \[
240	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
241	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
242	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
243	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
244	  \]
245	  and a canonical braiding
246	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
247	  inherited from the corresponding data on
248	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
249	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
254	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
255	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
256	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
257	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
258	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
259	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
260	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
261	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
262	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
263	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
264	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
265	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
266	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
267	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
268	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
269	\end{proof}
270	
271	\begin{theorem}
272	
273	\leanok
274	[Monoidal-category structure on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
275	  \label{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}
276	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory}
277	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
278	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
279	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, with unit object
280	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), associator \(\alpha\), left unitor \(\lambda\),
281	  right unitor \(\rho\), and braiding \(\beta\) of
282	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, satisfies the
283	  pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms of a symmetric monoidal
284	  category. Consequently \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) carries a
285	  canonical symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure.
286	\end{theorem}
287	
288	\begin{proof}
289	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
290	  % NOTE (plan iter-204): there is deliberately NO proof-block  here.
291	  % The Lean instance AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory has a
292	  % `:= sorry` body (intentional contamination guard; deferred-large monoidal
293	  % coherence, to be transported through the sheafification-forget reflective
294	  % adjunction). A proof-block  would assert "proof closed, no sorry",
295	  % which is false. The erroneous proof-block  that the deterministic
296	  % sync_leanok failed to remove across iter-203 was stripped here by the plan
297	  % agent as a correction of an over-claiming marker. The statement-block
298	  %  (the sorry-bodied decl exists) is correct and untouched.
299	  The pentagon and triangle identities (associator and unitor coherence)
300	  are inherited from the analogous identities on
301	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\). On any affine open
302	  \(\Spec A \subset X\), the assertions reduce to the analogous
303	  identities for the tensor product of \(A\)-modules, which are
304	  standard. The gluing data on overlaps is preserved by all four
305	  natural isomorphisms by construction, so the affine-level coherence
306	  identities yield global coherence identities on
307	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Symmetry of \(\beta\) follows by the
308	  same affine descent argument.
309	
310	  \emph{Concrete Mathlib realisation of this descent.} Rather than
311	  discharging pentagon/triangle/hexagon by hand, the coherence is
312	  transported through the sheafification localization. The category
313	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is the localization of
314	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) at the morphism property
315	  \(W := J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R)\)
316	  inverted by sheafification --- the instance
317	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification.IsLocalization}\,W\) is
318	  present in Mathlib. The generic transport
319	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\) (the instance
320	  \(\mathtt{instMonoidalCategoryLocalizedMonoidal}\)) produces a
321	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) on the localized category from a localization
322	  functor that inverts a \emph{monoidal} morphism property, i.e.\ from
323	  a \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance together with
324	  the \(\mathtt{IsLocalization}\) instance above. The single missing
325	  ingredient is therefore the \(\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for
326	  the relative \(\otimes_R\) on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\)
327	  (Mathlib's existing \(\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instances on the sites
328	  side concern the ambient/cartesian tensor on \(\mathtt{Sheaf}\,J\,A\),
329	  not the relative module tensor, so they do not apply). Once
330	  \(\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\,W\) is built, the same localization-monoidal
331	  transport simultaneously supplies the compatibility of \(\otimes_X\)
332	  with pullback/restriction (the strong-monoidal pullback iso
333	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} reduces to), so it is
334	  the highest-leverage single target for closing the whole substrate.
335	\end{proof}
336	
337	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
338	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
339	
340	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
341	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
342	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
343	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
344	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section spells out how the
345	monoidal-category structure of
346	\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to that subtype, and how
347	the restriction interacts with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\).
348	
349	\begin{lemma}
350	
351	\leanok
352	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
353	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
355	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
356	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
357	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
358	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
359	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
360	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
361	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
362	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
363	\end{lemma}
364	
365	\begin{proof}
366	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
367	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
368	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
369	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
370	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
371	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
372	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
373	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
374	\end{proof}
375	
376	\begin{lemma}
377	
378	\leanok
379	[Inverse of an invertible module]
380	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
381	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
382	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
383	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
384	  bundle. The dual
385	  \[
386	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
387	  \]
388	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
389	  isomorphism
390	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
391	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
392	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
393	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
394	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
395	\end{lemma}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
399	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
400	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
401	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
402	  The contraction map
403	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
404	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
405	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
406	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
407	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
408	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
409	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
410	\end{proof}
411	
412	\begin{lemma}
413	
414	\leanok
415	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
416	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
417	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
418	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
419	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
420	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
421	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
422	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
423	  \[
424	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
425	    \;\;=\;\;
426	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
427	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
428	  \]
429	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
430	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
431	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
432	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
433	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
434	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
435	\end{lemma}
436	
437	\begin{proof}
438	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
439	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
440	  Direct from
441	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
442	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
443	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
444	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
445	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
446	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
447	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
448	  monoidal-category structure of
449	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to the subcategory by
450	  the same affine descent argument.
451	\end{proof}
452	
453	\begin{lemma}
454	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
455	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
456	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
457	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
458	  functor
459	  \[
460	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
461	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
462	  \]
463	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
464	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
465	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
466	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
467	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
468	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
469	  canonical isomorphism
470	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
471	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
472	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
473	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
474	\end{lemma}
475	
476	\begin{proof}
477	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
478	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
479	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
480	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
481	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
482	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
483	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
484	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the
485	  same descent argument as in
486	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}. The compatibility with the
487	  unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
488	  same cover.
489	\end{proof}
490	
491	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
492	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
493	
494	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
495	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
496	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
497	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
498	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
499	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
500	
501	\begin{theorem}
502	
503	\leanok
504	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
505	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
507	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
508	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
509	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
510	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
511	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
512	
513	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
514	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
515	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
516	  % groups)
517	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
518	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
519	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
520	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
521	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
522	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
523	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
524	  % \end{dfn}
525	  %  [\dots]
526	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
527	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
528	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
529	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
530	  % topologies  by
531	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
532	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
533	  %  \end{dfn}"
534	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
535	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
536	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
537	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
538	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
539	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
540	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
541	  \[
542	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
543	  \]
544	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
545	  abelian-group structure with addition
546	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
547	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
548	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
549	  inverse
550	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
551	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
552	  supplied by the substrate of
553	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricted to the
554	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier via
555	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
556	  map
557	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
558	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
559	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
560	\end{theorem}
561	
562	\begin{proof}
563	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
564	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
565	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
566	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
567	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
568	  By
569	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible},
570	  the substrate restricts to an abelian-group structure on the set
571	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T / \sim\) of isomorphism
572	  classes of line bundles on \(C \times_k T\). Concretely:
573	  associativity and commutativity come from the monoidal-category
574	  axioms of \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} (pentagon, triangle,
575	  hexagon coherence on isomorphism classes); the neutral element is
576	  the class of \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (left/right unitor
577	  coherence); and the inverse is the class of
578	  \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C
579	  \times_k T})\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).
580	
581	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback
582	  functor
583	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
584	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) is a strong monoidal
585	  functor; in particular it carries products to products and preserves
586	  the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism classes
587	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
588	  homomorphism. Its image is therefore a subgroup of
589	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\).
590	
591	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set
592	  of \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of right cosets of this
593	  subgroup. The quotient by a subgroup of an abelian group is itself
594	  an abelian group, and the quotient map is a surjective group
595	  homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup --- the standard
596	  fact (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the
597	  general quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra
598	  textbook). Putting the pieces together gives the
599	  \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the
600	  statement, and identifies it with the structure of
601	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
602	\end{proof}

# Blueprint Writer Directive — Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex (flat/line-bundle pivot)

## Chapter to edit
`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex` (ONLY this file).

## Strategy context (the slice that matters)
A mathlib-analogist api-alignment audit (iter-206, persistent rationale at
`analogies/ts-design206.md` — READ IT FIRST, in full) found that this
chapter's current substrate is **over-built**. The chapter currently routes
the relative Picard group law through a FULL symmetric
`MonoidalCategory (Scheme.Modules X)` instance on ALL quasi-coherent
modules (`thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`), whose only remaining gap is a
verified-absent Mathlib instance `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)`
(needed for `whiskerLeft` stability of the sheafification-localizing
morphism property, for an ARBITRARY module `F`). That is a multi-file
Mathlib-PR-scale dead-end and must NOT be the chapter's route.

**The corrected mathematics**: the relative Picard group law is the group
of isomorphism classes of LINE BUNDLES under tensor. The group axioms on
iso-classes are *propositions* (`Nonempty (… ≅ …)`): they need NO monoidal
coherence (pentagon/triangle/hexagon) and NO closed structure. They need
exactly four existence-of-iso facts on line bundles — associativity
`(L⊗M)⊗N ≅ L⊗(M⊗N)`, unit `O_X⊗L ≅ L`, commutativity `L⊗M ≅ M⊗L`, and
inverse `L⊗L⁻¹ ≅ O_X` — plus well-definedness on iso-classes. The single
genuinely-hard ingredient is the *global* compatibility iso
`tensorObj_restrict_iso` (local triviality alone does NOT give a global
associator — two sheaves locally `≅ O` need not be globally iso, cf.
`O(1)` vs `O`); but for LINE BUNDLES, which are flat, this reduces to
**elementary flat-exactness**, NOT internal-hom machinery.

This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group `CommRing.Pic R` (in
`Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`): `CommGroup` transported from
`Units (Skeleton (ModuleCat R))` over a *fixed* ring, with the Prop
invertibility carrier `Module.Invertible R M` — which this project's
`IsLocallyTrivial` already mirrors verbatim (see
`LineBundlePullback.lean:106`). Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic` uses
`ModuleCat.monoidalCategory`/`SymmetricCategory` and needs NO
sheafification, NO localization, NO `MonoidalClosed`.

## Required edits (rewrite, do not just append)

1. **Demote/replace `thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`** (the global
   `MonoidalCategory (Scheme.Modules X)` instance, L271–335). The global
   monoidal-category instance on all modules is NO LONGER on the critical
   path and must not be presented as the substrate the group law rests on.
   Replace its body (especially the "Concrete Mathlib realisation"
   paragraph L310–335 that invokes `MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal W` /
   `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal` / the implicit
   `MonoidalClosed`) with the flat-scoped account. Either (preferred)
   convert it into a remark that the full all-modules monoidal category is
   NOT required and is explicitly off the critical path (with one sentence
   on why: coherence is never consumed by a group law on iso-classes), OR
   restate the theorem so its content is only "the line-bundle subcategory
   carries the iso-level operations" — your call, but the chapter must no
   longer claim the group law DEPENDS on a global `MonoidalCategory`/
   `MonoidalClosed`.

2. **Introduce the key ingredient `tensorObj_restrict_iso`, flat-scoped.**
   Add a lemma block (give it a `\label` like
   `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, no `\lean{}` pin unless you are certain of
   the final Lean name — leave it unpinned and note the target name
   `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso` in prose):
   for line bundles (equivalently flat modules) `L, M` and an open
   immersion / restriction `f`, the canonical map
   `(L ⊗_X M)|_f ≅ L|_f ⊗ M|_f` is an isomorphism, BECAUSE flat ⇒ `lTensor`
   preserves injectivity (`Module.Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap`,
   packaged in Mathlib as `Module.Invertible.lTensor_bijective_iff`,
   `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`) and `⊗` is always right-exact (preserves
   surjectivity). State explicitly that this is the elementary flat-exactness
   replacement for the deleted `MonoidalClosed`/`IsMonoidal W` route, and that
   `Module.Invertible R M ⇒ Module.Projective ⇒ flat` (PicardGroup.lean
   `instProjectiveOfInvertible`) makes the flatness automatic for line
   bundles.

3. **State the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas on line bundles**
   (associativity, unit `O_X⊗L≅L`, commutativity, inverse — the inverse is
   already `lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible`, keep it). Each is a
   `Nonempty (… ≅ …)`-style existence statement assembled from
   `tensorObj_restrict_iso` on a common trivialising cover. Make explicit
   that these four facts (not a `MonoidalCategory` instance) are what the
   group structure consumes.

4. **Rewrite the proof of `thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj`**
   (L562–602) so the group axioms are derived from the four
   existence-of-iso lemmas (propositions on iso-classes), NOT from
   "the monoidal-category axioms of `thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`
   (pentagon, triangle, hexagon coherence)". Keep the `QuotientAddGroup`
   assembly. Cite the Mathlib idiom alignment: the construction mirrors
   `CommRing.Pic = Units (Skeleton …)` / `instCommGroupPic`, transported to
   the scheme-level line-bundle subcategory (scheme Pic is genuinely absent
   from Mathlib — PicardGroup.lean TODO lists "connect to invertible
   sheaves" as unbuilt — so the project supplies it lightly).

5. **Update the Mathlib API survey (§`sec:tensorobj_api_survey`) and
   motivation** so the recorded "gap" is the flat-scoped
   `tensorObj_restrict_iso` (elementary, Mathlib-backed) rather than a
   global monoidal-category lift. Remove the framing that a global
   `MonoidalCategory`/`MonoidalClosed` on all modules is the missing piece.

6. **Update the LOC/sequencing section (§`sec:tensorobj_loc_estimates`)**:
   Piece 2 is no longer "discharge pentagon/triangle/hexagon"; it is
   "flat-scoped `tensorObj_restrict_iso` + the four iso lemmas". Re-estimate
   honestly (this is LIGHTER than the deleted route — likely ~80–150 LOC for
   the whole substrate, not 200–320).

## Keep unchanged
- The existing Kleiman §2 `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` blocks (df:aPf,
  df:Pfs) — they remain the correct math source for the group-valued
  relative Picard functor. Preserve them verbatim.
- `lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`, `lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible`,
  `lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct`, `lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj`,
  `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`, `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`
  — these are already line-bundle-correct; adjust their proof prose only
  where it appeals to the deleted global coherence.
- `IsLocallyTrivial` as the `Module.Invertible`-shaped Prop predicate.

## Out of scope (do NOT touch)
- Any other chapter. Representability (A.2.c), identity component (A.3),
  Albanese (A.4) framing stays as-is.
- Do NOT add or remove `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers anywhere — markers
  are managed by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase and the review
  agent, not by you.
- Do NOT introduce a `MonoidalClosed`/`IsMonoidal W` discharge plan; that
  route is explicitly rejected.

## References
- `analogies/ts-design206.md` (the design rationale — primary input).
- Mathlib: `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup` (`CommRing.Pic`,
  `instCommGroupPic`, `Module.Invertible`,
  `Module.Invertible.lTensor_bijective_iff`, the Projective/flat instances);
  `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.{Basic,Symmetric}`;
  `Module.Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap`. You may verify these
  via the Lean LSP. If you need a math source not already in `references/`,
  a child reference-retriever is authorized (Stacks 01CR is the Picard-group
  reference and is already cited in the chapter).
